narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, LSAT score of 32, Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at 3rd decile (below the typical 5th or 6th decile averages), and your plan to attend full-time—and calculated a 43.6% probability that you would fail the bar exam. Because this risk threshold was too high, your application was rejected to prioritize candidates with stronger predicted odds of bar passage success. Among all the factors considered, your LSAT score had the single strongest influence, working strongly in your favor by pushing the prediction toward passing, even though your 32 is below the average of 36.7 seen in past students. Next in importance was your Year 3 decile ranking of 3, which had a notable negative pull, nudging the model toward predicting failure since it signals performance in the lower third of your school's peers, compared to the average around the middle of the pack. Your middle family income bracket came third in influence, providing a moderate boost toward the passing side, likely reflecting stability that helps many students like you succeed. Overall, while your LSAT and income offered key strengths, the Year 3 grades tipped the balance enough against you when weighed with your other academics, like that below-average undergrad GPA. This placed your profile among those with elevated bar risk, unlike typical admitted students who cluster around higher deciles and scores. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially as a full-time aspiring lawyer from a background where hard work often pays off. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that top favorable factor, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors at schools that value growth stories like yours. Keep pushing—you've got the determination it takes.
